edit_steps.instruction: Add an animated starfield/space background clip as the base layer for the intro
Add a centered bold white text title reading 'CAPCUT MOBILE OR DESKTOP?'
Replace or add the next title card text 'THINKING UP IDEAS FOR YOUR BRAND' in the same centered style
Replace or add the next title card text 'UNDERSTANDING THE USER INTERFACE' in the same centered style
Create a new project from the home screen.
Name the project with a descriptive title and a date prefix.
Import the video, audio, and image files into the asset window using the import/plus button or by dragging from your computer.
Add the beach clip to the timeline by dragging it onto the desired layer.
Stack a second clip on a layer above the main layer to demonstrate top-down layer order.
Scale down the top overlay clip so the layer beneath becomes visible.
Set an initial scale keyframe on the clip.
Move the playhead forward and set a second scale keyframe to zoom in over time.
Drag the two keyframes closer together to make the zoom animation faster.
Enable the auto-snapping toggle on the timeline.
Trim the clip by dragging its left edge inward with the select tool.
Use the split tool to cut the clip at the playhead.
Select the unwanted segment and delete it.
Step frame-by-frame with the arrow keys to the exact frame where your feet hit the ground.
Add a marker (M) at the frame where your feet hit the ground.
On the incoming house clip, use the split-and-delete-left tool (Q) to remove everything before the jump landing.
Drag the trimmed house clip so its start snaps to the marker, aligning the transition timing.
If the marker is mispositioned, right-click it and delete it, then re-add a marker (M) at the correct landing frame.
Scale up the house clip to raise the feet position so it matches the beach clip framing.
Use the split-and-delete-right tool (W) to remove unneeded trailing footage on the remaining clips.
Search the audio/sound-effects library for a 'thump' effect and add it to the timeline.
Align the sound effect's peak waveform with the transition point using the marker.
Open the music library, choose a travel track, and drag it onto the timeline.
Drag the music so a high-energy peak lines up with the transition hit.
Lower the thump sound-effect volume so it stops clipping and stays subtle.
Drag the music clip fully to the left to remove dead space at the start.
Extract audio from a clip you only need for its sound, then delete the audio or adjust its volume as needed.
Create a new project and import your selected clips in the order you tap them.
Disable the auto-added default ending in settings by checking 'discard ending'.
Arrange clips into the correct order by long-pressing and dragging them in the timeline.
Trim the front of the first clip to start on a point of action (foot in the air).
For the match cut, position the marker where the sandals first touch the ground, select the clip, split, and delete the right side.
On the second clip, move the marker one frame past the contact point, split, and delete the left side so the motion matches seamlessly.
Trim off the unused back portion of the second clip.
Demonstrate the alternate layered method: split the first clip at the contact point but keep both parts.
Select the second clip, find its start point, split, and delete the unwanted portion.
With the second clip selected, tap Overlay to move it onto a separate layer above the main track.
Lower the overlay clip's opacity to see both shots, then reposition the overlay so the feet line up with the lower layer.
Adjust the overlay clip's position by dragging it in the preview to align the feet.
Raise the overlay clip's opacity back to 100%.
Trim both layers so they end at the same point: split the lower clip at the chosen end and delete the remainder, then split the overlay at the same point and delete its right side.
Mute the main track audio using the clip-audio mute toggle if you don't need the original sound.
Open the Audio tab and go into Sound Effects; search 'thump' for a transition hit sound.
Add the chosen thump sound effect; it drops at the marker, then slide it to align with the transition.
Lower the sound effect volume to about 30%.
Go to Audio > Sounds, browse the music library, and add a track with a slow buildup that breaks on the transition.
Drag the music clip onto its layer and trim the front so the music beat lines up with the transition, then extend it to the left edge.
Reduce the music track volume to a balanced level so the audio doesn't clip.
Trim the music to end with the video: split it offset from the end, delete the excess, and let snap pull it to the clip end.
Apply a fade in and fade out (about 0.3-0.5s) to the music for a smoother ending.
Add your primary footage as the base layer at the bottom of the timeline.
Stack a second clip as an overlay layer on top of the base layer.
Apply a split mask to the top layer to reveal the bottom layer where the mask is inactive.
Switch the mask to a rectangle shape and size it smaller so the bottom layer shows around it.
Add a third overlay layer of a subject shot on a green screen.
Key out the green background or use the cutout module to remove the background from the subject.
Move the playhead to the start point and set a position keyframe on the subject.
Move the playhead forward and reposition the subject to automatically create a second keyframe, animating it across the frame.
Add a black layer as the base.
Overlay a white layer directly on top of the black layer.
Apply a split mask to the white layer, showing white on top and black on the bottom.
Rotate the mask to make the split diagonal.
Change the mask to a circle and move it to window through to the top layer.
Click Invert to reverse the mask and reveal the bottom layer through the circle.
Increase the feather to soften the mask edge for a smooth transition.
Change the mask to a rectangle and click Invert again to show the white layer on top.
To blend two clips side by side, apply a split mask, rotate it, and add feather to smooth the seam.

recipes.title: Make an Object Appear
filming_steps: We're gonna make an object appear just by snapping our fingers.
For this we're gonna need two shots.
The first shot we're gonna snap our fingers, pick up the cup and take a drink and then the second shot we're gonna get with the cup removed from the scene that will create our clean plate.
Let's go ahead and do our focus lock and hit record.
Shot one then our second shot, we just want our clean plate no cup in the scene.

One thing to know is that when you have natural lighting coming into the window, this can be problematic if we have the sun shifting or clouds moving during the day and you wanna make sure to get your shots just back to back.
Basically not giving the sun enough time to shift and change the lighting in the scene.

edit_steps.instruction: Import the long clip and place it on the timeline.
Split off the excess footage at the beginning before the action and delete it.
Split the hero clip at the point where the glass is set down to mark the end of shot one.
Split to isolate a few seconds of clean-plate footage where the countertop has no cup.
Delete the middle and trailing excess so only the action clip and the clean-plate clip remain.
Select the clean-plate clip and tap Overlay to move it onto its own layer (layer 2).
Long-press the overlay clip and drag it all the way to the start of the timeline.
Shorten the overlay clip to keep it manageable.
With the overlay selected, open the Mask tool and choose the Rectangle shape.
Position and shrink the rectangle mask to cover the cup without overlapping your body.
Adjust the feather to blend the mask edges into the underlying clip, keeping it minimal so it doesn't reveal the cup.
Set the overlay clip's opacity to 100%.
Trim the end of the overlay (patch) clip to the exact point where the fingers snap so the cup is revealed.
Mute both clips to remove duplicate audio.
Select the clip and correct the warm/yellow cast using white balance temperature, finding a neutral middle ground where whites read white without the walls going too blue.
Under HSL, select the orange channel and pull down its saturation to reduce remaining yellow-orange, keeping the effect subtle.

filming_steps: I think what I wanna do is I wanna hide behind the trees.
Wanna hide behind the trees over there.
Let's lock this screen on the left side over here.
Our exposure stays locked right there.
We'll hit record.
I'll be this way.

Just to see how our placement looks in the scene.
Let's go ahead and review that.
You can see the clouds are already moving fast so this is gonna be easy.
All right.
This little work I can actually stay closer to camera.
What I'll do is I'll stay standing and do my action there.
Not loving how much grass is showing on the bottom frame.
Let's work on our framing a little bit.
Maybe a little more like this.
Go ahead and lock our exposure again and at this time I'm gonna let the camera continue to record after I move out of frame.

Run straight out and snap.
Let's try that again.

Run out right here.

Just to note that the slower the clouds are moving the longer that you wanna record so that you can speed that up and get really fast time lapse while you're standing still in the scene.

edit_steps.instruction: Drag the long take onto the timeline.
Zoom out to view the entire clip.
Duplicate the clip and place the copy on an overlay track below, aligning the start so the timings match.
Find the finger-snap moment and split the top clip exactly at the snap.
Select the long background portion and increase its speed dramatically (around 80x) to sell the time-lapse.
Mute the sped-up background clip.
Match the foreground clip's timing to the background: split both at the start point and delete the front portions so they align.
On the top foreground clip, open Remove Background > Custom removal and use the smart brush to paint over yourself and the trees (Pro tool).
Apply the cutout so the foreground covers the time-lapse background.
Free alternative: duplicate the top clip, turn off custom removal, and open the Mask panel.
Apply a horizontal mask along the treeline and reverse/invert it so the foreground is kept.
Add a small amount of feather (~3) to blend the mask edge along the top of the trees.
Add a second circle mask, widen and rotate it to fill in the remaining trees, and add slight feather (~2).
Trim the end: split the clip, delete the right side, then drag the lower clip so both ends match up.
Start the effect later: split, delete the left sides, and slide the timing back to the left so the clips align.
Toggle the bottom layer's eyeball to preview the foreground and fine-tune mask placement and feather as needed.
Deselect everything and play back to review the final time-warp effect.

recipes.title: Intro to Keyframes
filming_steps: So we have our AV lock so that we don't get any shifts in lighting gonna go ahead and hit record.
For this transition I'm gonna use this pool noodle cause it's clear to see.
The idea is that we're gonna drop the noodle.
You can use any stick for this and as we drop it it's gonna reveal our second shot.

I'm gonna change from my red shirt to my blue shirt and what's gonna happen is we're gonna have it up here we're gonna set keyframes and a mask and then as we as it drops our mask is gonna follow it and reveal our new shirt behind.
So one thing that I wanna make sure I'm in the center and I have my feet planted and I'm gonna keep my shoes in the same spot.
Let's try that again.
I'm gonna drop my arms down to my sides so that I can easily match my second shot.
So what I'm gonna do is I'm gonna take my shoes off so that I know exactly where I'm standing and I end up in the same spot.
Change my shirt real quick okay so now I'm gonna go and put my feet back in my shoes and at least I know that I'm standing in the same spot so it helps that much with transition with the alignment.
This will just be our second shot and it's just a reaction I'm not gonna touch the needle.
I'll stand here for a couple seconds to get safety shot and there we have it.
My shirt's blue.

edit_steps.instruction: Place the white layer below the black layer as an overlay so it sits on top in the preview.
At the end of the clip, open opacity and set a keyframe.
Go to the start of the clip and pull opacity down to zero.
Move the keyframes closer together to make the opacity animation faster.
Add a final keyframe at the end and pull opacity down to create a smooth fade out.
Delete the opacity keyframes to reset the clip.
Scale the white layer down and set a keyframe at the start.
Go to the end of the clip and scale the layer back up to animate a scale-up.
Add a middle keyframe scaled up and an end keyframe scaled down for an A-B-C scale animation.
Delete the scale keyframes to reset.
Delete the white layer and place a sticker on top of the black layer.
Set a keyframe on the sticker and scale it up so it's visible.
Go to the end and rotate the sticker (use the basic tab rotation for precise numbers) to animate a spin.
At the end keyframe, also scale down and reposition the sticker to animate scale, position, and rotation together.
Delete the sticker.
Add a text layer to reveal.
At the end of the text clip, set a keyframe.
Go to the beginning and drag the text out of frame to animate a fly-in.
Move the keyframes closer together to speed up the fly-in.
Open the curves button next to the keyframe and apply an ease (ease out) for a smoother transition.
Trim shot 1 (red shirt) and shot 2 (blue shirt reaction) to the needed sections.
Select the first clip (the one with the moving stick) and tap overlay to drop it onto its own layer, and move it to the top layer.
Add a mask and choose the split (horizontal) mask.
Tap invert to flip the mask so the correct shot is revealed underneath.
Position the playhead at the middle hero frame and place the mask line right at the top of the pool noodle.
Add a small amount of feather to the mask edge.
Add the first mask keyframe.
Step forward ~4 frames at a time, moving and rotating the mask to follow the stick, adding keyframes as you go.
Work backwards from the hero frame, stepping 4 frames at a time and keyframing the mask above the object until it clears the hand.
Slip the red shirt layer left and trim as needed to align with the reaction.
Extend the blue clip later to match the drop timing.
Split the blue clip at the reaction moment to use as a timing marker.
Trim and delete the unneeded clip ends (split first, then delete).
Fine-tune the mask position within the transition, then deselect and review.

recipes.title: Object Reveal
filming_steps: I'm actually gonna walk the focus on the carpet where I'll be standing.
Just to make sure that we're nice and sharp.
This one's a pretty simple move basically just gonna walk across screen and we're gonna reveal the text or logo right behind us.

I'm getting a couple different shots so that I can choose my favorite and I'm basically acting like as if there was text on the wall right here so I'm gonna use my eyeline and look at the spot where I want the text to be.
Go ahead and walk in and as the text reveals I'm looking at it.
For this one that's all we need just the one shot and let's take it inside the edit and I'll show you how we layer it together.

edit_steps.instruction: Import your chosen walk-across shot into the timeline.
Add a sticker (logo/object) to reveal; choose a non-animated one like the follow/subscribe sticker since text errors out.
Stretch the sticker across the full duration of the clip.
Scale and position the sticker, baking in its final size now.
Export this clip with the sticker baked in, then reimport the exported file.
Duplicate your original clip to keep it for later changes.
Place the exported baked-sticker clip on top of the original clip, aligning their matching in/out points.
Duplicate the original clip again and place it on the top layer, creating a sandwich: original / baked-sticker / original.
On the top layer, apply Remove Background > Auto removal to cut yourself out; wait for processing.
Select the middle layer and add a horizontal split mask.
Rotate the mask so only the right side is visible, and add a little feather to smooth the edge.
Position the mask line at the middle of your body in the current (middle) frame and set a keyframe on the mask.
Go to the end of the clip and move the mask to the middle of your body to auto-set an end keyframe.
Go to the start of the clip and move the mask to your body to auto-set a beginning keyframe.
Scrub between keyframes and add corrective keyframes where the mask drifts off your body until tracking is accurate.
If the reveal plays inverted, select the masked layer and hit Reverse on the mask to flip which side shows.
Verify the timing of all three clips matches; if layers get out of order, use Basic > Layer order to set the masked clip to layer 2 and the cutout above.
Deselect and play back to confirm the object reveals as you walk past.

recipes.title: BONUS CONTENT

So I'll hit record And I'm gonna come over here and stand in the center of my frame And what I'm gonna do is just a little bit of a jump so that the impact when I come down helps the transition feel a little bit More natural and then I'll save my hook Here's how to create this piece try again.
Here's how to create this appearing effect using just your phone Okay, so we got that and what I'm gonna do is just step out of the way So now what we're doing is recording our clean plate.

Just let that roll for a few seconds Okay, so we'll stop recording and we'll take these clips into our editor and I'll show you how to cut them together to make that effect happen Okay, so to create this clothing transition, we really just need to record one long shot.
I'm going to start out with my jacket on, and I'm going to do my motion, say my line.
Just snap my fingers, and then I'll take the jacket off and do the same thing.
I like to do that because if we're trying to stay in the same position, it's a lot harder to put the jacket on and stay in position than it is to take the jacket off.
That's a trick.
It is like doing the two shots in reverse that I like to do, and it makes it a lot more seamless.
We'll lock our exposure and hit record, ramming here.

Here's how to change your clothes at the snap of a finger.

I'll do a quick review of my shots and just make sure that I got what I need.
I'm really just trying to pay attention where the framing of my body is.
Stop on the frame.
I can just kind of look at where the frame of my body is.
If I stop at a certain point, I can just look at when I snap my fingers kind of where my head and shoulders are.
All right, looks good.
So to create this cool glitch effect, we're just gonna record one long shot We're just gonna stand in different positions and in the last position.

We're just gonna say our line or our opening hook So for this one, I'm not gonna do my exposure lock because I don't really care if the lighting changes It's gonna jump so fast that it won't make a difference So I'll go ahead and hit record And I could use my reverse camera here to see my phone, but I have my external monitor down here that I so I can see So I can see where I'm standing So I'll just stand in a few different positions for a few seconds come over here.

We can make it kind of funny too And I'm trying to go left to right back and forth because the jump will feel a lot more abrupt and really grab attention And I want to make it seem like I'm getting ready to talk that will also create a little bit of curiosity And then the last one I don't want to be too close, but I'm gonna go ahead and save my line Here's how to create this glitch effect using just your phone I'll do a few different options, too Here's how to create this glitch effect using just your phone Here's how to create this jump cut effect using just your phone Here's how to create this jump cut effect using just your phone Here's how to create this jump cut effect using just your phone Here's how to create this jump cut effect using just your phone Okay so I'm pretty happy with those but just for safety I'm gonna go ahead and get a few more options.
I'm gonna start here.
There's one, three, here's how to create this jump cut effect using just your phone.

If I was outside I could make the distance a lot farther so that jump cut will feel a lot more abrupt because there's a lot more distance being traveled but if you shorten the clips enough you'll get the same effect here.

edit_steps.instruction: Import the widescreen stomp test clip and add it to the timeline.
Set the project aspect ratio to vertical 9:16 for short-form content.
Select the clip, split off the front, and delete the lead-in so it starts right at the action.
Split off the end after the look-up and delete it to remove dead space.
Zoom into the timeline for precision.
Reset the clip's position to 0,0 to center it in the frame.
Add markers (press M) at each stomp/foot impact, using the audio spikes as reference, marking all five hits.
At the first stomp, go two frames before the marker and set a position keyframe to lock the starting position.
At the first stomp marker, set a position keyframe dropping the clip down by 60 pixels (Y = -60).
Two frames before the second stomp, set a keyframe locking position at -60.
At the second stomp marker, set a keyframe moving the clip to -120 (another 60-pixel nudge).
Two frames before the third stomp, set a keyframe locking position at -120.
At the third stomp marker, set a keyframe moving the clip to -180 for the third nudge.
For the first big jump, two frames before the marker set a keyframe to lock position, then at the marker drop it far down (e.g. -600) as an even number.
For the second big jump, two frames before set a keyframe locking at -600, then at the marker move it to roughly -1200 for an extreme drop.
Add a text layer for the hook, type the first line (e.g. 'This is why your views are so low'), and set it to all caps.
Snap the text to the end of the clip, enlarge it, and position it centered.
Duplicate the text (hold Option/Alt and drag) to create three stacked lines, then position one to the top and one to the bottom.
Adjust text layer start times so the text begins revealing on the third tap, lining up reveals with the downward movements.
Add a fourth smaller centered text line as a call to action (e.g. 'Read the caption'), kept above the crop-safe area.
Insert a copied emoji/arrow into the call-to-action text by pasting it from an external app.
Duplicate the keyframed video clip (Option/Alt drag) and place the copy on a layer above the text, ensuring its timing/end frame lines up exactly with the original.
Fine-tune the keyframe spacing and reveal positions so the text uncovers progressively as desired.
Import the base 'before' costume clip and place it on the main timeline track
Import the 'after' costume clip and add it as an overlay layer above the base clip
Split the base clip at the moment the costume change should trigger
Remove the background from the costume overlay using the free alternative background removal tool
Apply the layer overlay feature to blend the costume layer over the base footage
Set the costume layer to fly in by adding position keyframes from off-screen to its final placement
Add a flashlight/light sweep overlay clip on a track above to mask the transition moment
Set the flashlight overlay blend mode to screen so the light reveals through
Keyframe the costume layer opacity to fade in as the flashlight sweeps across
Add a transition at the cut point to smooth the costume change reveal

recipes.title: Getting Started
filming_steps: Let's talk about shooting green screen and camera settings.
Exposure Adjust the camera settings to avoid over exposing or under exposing the green screen the goal is to capture a vibrant uniform green that can easily be heat up.
Focus Be mindful depth of field.
Subject should be in sharp focus while ensuring the green screen remains a consistent color throughout the scene.
Resolution In general you wanna have sharper footage to work with when pulling a key.
Frame Rate and Shutter Speed Motion blur can be problematic and cause less than appealing results.
Higher frame rates and shutter speed will help eliminate motion blur.
If your subject is moving around a lot you're gonna wanna opt for higher frame rates and shutter speeds 30 frames per second or higher.
If shooting with your phone you may want to use a third party app so you have full control over your settings.

Background and Foreground Integration Lens Selection Depending on the size of your green screen you'll need to consider the focal length of your lens if you have a small screen you typically need to place your camera farther back from the subject with a larger focal length to keep the subject visually within the screen.
If using your cell phone you might be able to just pull your tripod back and change it to 2 times zoom if you have a larger screen you'll have more flexibility and creative control.
Matching Elements When planning your shots consider how the subjects will interact with the intended background and post production.
This includes matching the direction of the lighting, the color temperature and perspective of the camera.
If you can use the same lighting setup for both scenes for a seamless integration.

edit_steps.instruction: Set camera exposure to capture a vibrant, uniform green without over- or under-exposing the screen.
Set focus and depth of field so the subject is sharp while the green screen stays a consistent color.
Shoot at a high resolution to get sharper footage for pulling a clean key.
Use a high frame rate (30fps or higher) and fast shutter speed to eliminate motion blur, especially for moving subjects.
If shooting on a phone, use a third-party camera app for full manual control of settings.
Select lens focal length to keep the subject within the screen; pull the camera back or use 2x zoom for small screens.
Match lighting direction, color temperature, and camera perspective between the subject and the intended background.
Choose a wrinkle-resistant, bright chroma key green screen sized to cover the full frame with room for subject movement.
Position the subject at least 6 feet from the green screen to minimize shadows and green spill.
Light the green screen evenly with soft diffuse sources placed symmetrically at 45-degree angles on both sides.
Light the subject separately using a 3-point setup to control shadows and avoid green spill.
Add back or rim light to create separation between the subject and the background.
Use flags to block stray light from the screen and reflectors to fill subject shadows without affecting screen lighting.
For outdoor shoots, shoot on overcast days, use the sun as a backlight with reflectors as fill, and secure gear with sandbags and clamps.
Import the background footage and the green screen book image onto the timeline.
Place the green screen book image as an overlay layer above the background.
Extend the book layer to match the length of the background clip.
Select the book layer, open Remove Background, and enable Chroma Key.
Use the color picker to sample a mid-range green from the background.
Slowly raise the chroma key intensity until the green is removed, backing off before it removes blues.
Increase the shadow value to fill in the reflective blue areas that were keyed out.
Open Mask and apply a rectangle mask to crop out the visible borders around the book.
Add a small amount of feather (around 2) to smooth the mask edges.
Tighten the mask edges, especially the top, so the feather doesn't reveal leftover background.
Scale the book layer down so it roughly matches the size of the books in the pile.
Go to the first frame and set a position keyframe with the book positioned over the pile.
Move to the end frame and reposition the book upward to set the ending position keyframe.
Shorten the book layer and align the keyframe to the layer end to tighten the float timing.
At the first frame, set a rotation keyframe on the book.
At the end frame, apply a rotation (around -130) to add an organic spin as it floats up.
With the book layer selected, open the Adjustment panel and shift the tint slightly toward purple to integrate it into the scene.
Add warmth and lower the brightness slightly so the book whites match the scene.
Create a global adjustment layer via Adjustment > Custom > plus, placed above all layers to affect both clips.
Scale up the background layer and reposition it so the scene reads closer and stays framed.
Increase the book scale and reposition it over the books while hovering on the existing keyframe to replace it.
At the first frame set a scale keyframe on the book, then at the end frame scale it up slightly for better legibility.
Export the project as the floating book demo render.
Re-import the exported render and place it on the timeline.
Set the in point (N) at the layer start and out point (O) at the last frame to define the render range.
At the first frame set a scale keyframe on the imported render.
At the end frame, set scale to about 110% to create a subtle fake camera push-in.

recipes.title: Flying Book
filming_steps: So for this effect we're gonna create a flying object as it's flying though it's gonna spin towards camera and it's gonna come back to us.
We're gonna act as if we're throwing it.
When we throw it it's gonna appear to spin towards camera.
Slow down so you get a good look at it and then it's gonna come back to us and we're going to catch it okay.

The way that we're gonna do this is we're gonna stand in one place we're gonna have the object in our hand and we're gonna throw it make it look like we're throwing it and we're gonna freeze for a second.

We're gonna have another shot that we're gonna take with the spinning object in front of a green screen and will composite that in have it scale up as it spins and most of this will come in the editing side but it will appear to come right in front of the camera and then as it comes back then we unfreeze and we catch it.

So a couple things that all note about my scene here I've killed the fluorescent lighting in the garage don't ever rely on fluorescent lighting for your projects it creates nasty flickering and the colors just really aren't that nice.

For instance right here we have a window with some natural light coming in so if that were to mix with like the yellow coming from the fluorescent it would just look really nasty our skin colors would not be correct and we wouldn't be able to edit it the way that we want to.

We wanna just really rely on one light source um right now for this tutorial I've actually closed off the garage and I have a big soft box right here just lighting me and the whole scene that's to make sure that you can see me well.

Another option that we could do for this video is I could just open up the garage and let the sunlight in and that would be like a nice natural light there that would just fill the scene it would remove a lot of the shadows and um wouldn't be too difficult okay.
Let's get to it okay.

Before I hit record on my phone what I'm gonna do is I'm gonna lock my focus point and exposure somewhere right here on the ground because that is in line with where I'm standing so we know that will be sharp and focus and the exposure won't shift over time okay.
I come over to my phone and I'll do is hold my finger right about where I'm gonna be standing doesn't have to be perfect but getting it locked is very important.
So that's locked we're gonna hit record.
Let's just take a couple practice shots at our throw.
Let's get our book and my center point just want to kind of know where my center point is.
A little mark on the ground there okay so I'll keep that in mind.

What I'm gonna do is I'm gonna create a little bit of a story out of this and then I can edit it how I want but I always wanna make sure that I'm leaving some type of beginning middle and end right so make sure that you can define those three things so that you can build a story throughout your project no matter how simple it is.

In this we would have me walking in and picking up a book right so there's a beginning then I'm going to look at the book look at the camera, throw the book there's the middle books gonna come back and we're gonna open it read it and walk off and there's the end.
If I do like a practice run right now.
If I threw this book the way that I wanted to move.
One two three one two three okay So about five six seconds right there.
Throw one two three one two three, okay.
It's better to have a little bit more time because you can speed it up rather than having to slow it down if it's too short.
So that's what I'm gonna do I'm gonna set the book down when I pick it up I'm gonna look at it and just throw it I'm gonna count as I kind of freeze in motion and then I'm gonna act as if I catch it.
What I'm gonna do when I throw it is I'm gonna hold it thin like this so that it looks like it's not in my hand and we can actually hide it behind the edit.
If I have that and then I put a book right there spinning it might hide it's gonna hide it from my hand right there okay.
1 2 1 2 3 Getting quite turn the book there let me try again.
2 3 1 2 3 Okay so that might be what we need I'll go ahead and do a couple different ones.
I did that one a little bit faster less time and I'll go and do 3.

So now for the next shot we're gonna shoot this book in front of a green screen okay so whenever compositing two shots together it's really good to match the lighting.

It's a lot easier to match the scene together rather than once one scene the lighting is coming from the left and the other scene the lighting is coming from the right and so forth.
What we're gonna do is take this guy.

Doesn't have to be perfect as long as it encompasses the entire book so you can see that with this big bright light it's just filling it up so this is nice and illuminated there's no harsh shadows there's no hotspots you know if I were to stand right here you can see there's a shadow so that is what we do not want.
We have to keep our object far enough ahead of it so that it's not casting any shadows.
I'm gonna go ahead and move my camera it doesn't have to match it's gonna be better if actually I just create a new scene here.

The lighting still matches and then what I'm gonna do is I'm gonna hook a string to it with some tape some fishing line so you can't see it and I'm just gonna hang it in front of the green screen and then we're gonna spin it.
I'll just go ahead and keep that recording I have.
Fishing line, tape, wire, cutters and this is pretty simple you can come up with your own contraption or use this.
Fishing line is good it's nice and see through and then probably put it somewhere in the middle of the book.
What you gotta do is you gotta so it doesn't slip out enough to have enough to tie it.

Let's put her tape down and then hopefully when we close it it's gonna keep that tight enough okay so what I did was I just made a little loop, tied a knot in the and then put the tape in between that so that it can't just slip out okay so there's a lot of different ways that you can do this I like fishing line it's very see through and it will remove once we cut the key the green out.
Careful slipping a little bit what I wanna do here is get rid of my exposure lock that we set on the ground.
We have a new object and I wanna make sure that it never goes out of the frame and it never leaves the frame of the green screen.

I'm gonna stop recording for a second so that I can have a separate shot to work with and what I'm gonna do is change it to 60 frames per second and this is so that it we can slow it down and kind of have more frames to work with when we're editing.

Ready and Slipping my fingers a little bit there too.
It is recording in slow motion so this one I'm gonna speed it up a little bit so we can slow it down.

Now that we have it wound up we can actually just let it unwind.
If you did have something to hang it from it would be a little bit more stable.
Ready and let's let it unwind.

edit_steps.instruction: Import the main throw clip into the timeline.
Position the playhead at the beginning of the usable take with a little extra and tap split, then delete the unused clip on the left.
Move to the end of the take, split with the clip selected, and delete the unused clip on the right.
Drag the clip edge to trim off some of the head of the clip.
Right as the throw begins, split the main clip to mark where the book starts flying.
Move forward to just before the catch and split the main clip there to mark the out point.
Bring the green screen book clip into the timeline as an overlay layer above the main clip.
Trim the green screen clip down to the smooth unwinding section from the middle of the spin.
With the green screen clip selected, tap reverse so the book spins in the correct direction.
Trim the green screen clip to capture two full rotations starting with the binding facing camera, leaving a little extra.
With the green screen clip selected, open the Video tab, choose Remove Background and select Chroma Key, then use the color picker to sample a mid-tone green from the screen.
Boost the chroma key intensity to about 9 to fully remove the green and the fishing line without damaging the book edges.
Increase the shadow setting to fill in remaining green holes around the book.
Open the Video tab, select Mask and choose Rectangle, sizing it to crop out the green screen edges while keeping the book inside.
Add a feather of about 2 to the mask to smooth the cropped edges.
Scrub through the clip to confirm the book never crosses the mask edges; tighten and shrink the mask top and bottom if any green remains.
Split the green screen clip into three parts: a fast spin-in section, a slow-motion readable section, and a fast spin-back section.
Select the middle slow-motion section and set its speed to 0.5x.
Speed up the first and last green screen sections to 2x to shorten them.
Adjust the speed settings further (slow the middle a bit more) and drag the sections to fully fill the marked in/out area with no gaps.
Set scale and position keyframes on the last frame of the first green screen section, typing scale 170.
Go to the first frame of that section, shrink the book scale down to about book size, and reposition it to auto-set a keyframe so it grows and moves into frame.
On the first frame of the last green screen section, type scale 170 and set scale and position keyframes.
Go to the last frame of that section and scale the book down to just larger than book size to create the fly-back shrink.
Hide the main layer and scrub through to verify the real book is concealed by the composited book the whole time, then turn the main layer back on.
Select all the green screen clips, right click and create a compound clip to nest them.
With the compound clip selected, add Motion Blur to smooth the spinning edges.
In the Audio tab, search whoosh and add a throw-style whoosh sound effect at the initial throw.
Duplicate the whoosh and place copies at the center slow-motion moment and right before the catch.
Adjust the whoosh speeds (0.5x and 1.5x variations) and nudge timing so the initial whoosh hits earlier on the throw and the second lands when slow motion starts.
Lower the volume of the two whoosh effects to about -3 dB for subtlety.
Go to Adjustments, choose Custom Adjustment, click the plus to add an adjustment layer on top, and extend it to the end of the timeline.
On the adjustment layer, boost exposure, lift shadows, and cool the warm tone to brighten and balance the scene.
Add a trending background music track and place it on the timeline.
Listen for the beat drop and split the music to mark that spot, trimming the intro so the beat drop lines up at the front when the throw happens.
Trim the music end to match where the video layers end.
Click Export at the top, add a version number, and save to the export folder.
Select the reaction clip with arms not spread too far and split it from the main footage
Shrink the split reaction clip down to isolate the short segment
Apply speed and slow the kick clip way down
Keep the kick clip as the base layer on the main timeline
Select the reaction section, split it, then tap overlay to drop it to a second layer
Drag the overlay clip all the way over to align with the kick point
Position the playhead at the exact moment the kick happens
Select the clip, tap split, and delete the right side
Select the freeze-frame reaction clip and tap overlay to drop it down a layer
Scoot the freeze-frame overlay all the way over to the end of the kick clip so it cuts from one to the other
On the freeze-frame clip, apply background removal using auto removal
Duplicate the kick clip
Tap overlay to drop the duplicate to a third layer
Drag the third-layer clip over to match the timing of the kick layer
Apply background removal with auto removal on the third-layer kick clip
Scoot the clip to align the kick foot with the split point
Trim the front of the clip and scoot everything back over to close the gap
At the moment it transitions to the cut-out shot, add a keyframe
Move the playhead forward and open the basic adjustment panel
Move the position up into the sky and scoot the subject over
Set the scale to zero at the end keyframe so the subject shrinks away
Trim the end of the clip
Remove the keyframe since the launch happens too slowly, then redo the adjustments over a shorter range
Set the Y position to send the subject up into the sky
Scale the subject way down to shrink it away
Select the clip, move to the end, tap motion blur and turn the blur all the way up
Hover over the first keyframe, open the curve graph, choose custom ease, and shape the curve to smooth the launch

recipes.title: Fly like Superman
filming_steps: To create a superhero flying effect we're gonna find a nice open piece of land to work with.
Let's rotate camera over here.
We're gonna run out into the middle right there.
We're gonna jump and we're gonna fly up into the sky.
The sky is absolutely beautiful here so we wanna make sure we get a nice composition showcasing those clouds in the sky.
I'm gonna lock my exposure on the ground around the area where I might be jumping from and I'll hit record and we'll do our test shot.
Usually I wanna try to grab some attention to the camera and I'll start here and run backwards.
Let's go back and see how this looks and then we'll make adjustments from there.

So one thing I'm noticing is that I'm pretty far away from the camera so we're gonna do it again and we'll use our marker this time so that we don't travel so far away from the camera and we'll just move it right about here.
I can see that that is too close so I'm thinking maybe more like right right there.
Do one more test shot.
See how that looks and make another adjustment if needed.
I'm happy with that composition but lock our exposure, hit record and I'll do a couple different takes so that I can choose my favorite and post.
Definitely traveled a little bit there I wanna stay as straight as possible.
I'm also gonna split up the clips by stopping and starting recording each time okay exposure is still locked.
I'm going to hit record and try another take.
I think I already like that take better.
Let's try one.
I'm running backwards.
I'm running forwards and we'll do one more.
It's gonna be a little bit more of a subtle action.
I think I should be looking up at the sky the whole time.
Come back and review the footage before moving the tripod.
That'll work.

edit_steps.instruction: Start a new project and import your footage.
Add your preferred take to the timeline.
Split the clip and delete the left portion.
Create a freeze frame at the jump point and drop it onto the timeline.
Duplicate the clip layer (copy and paste as overlay).
Move the duplicate to the third layer and line it up so timings match the source clip.
Select the cutout clip and apply Cutout > Remove Background.
Apply a rectangle mask to the cutout to isolate yourself from any trees or clouds pulled into the cutout.
Set a position keyframe on the cutout, move forward, and drag the figure up out of frame to animate the fly-up.
Go to the very end of the source clip and split it to isolate a clean patch section.
Overlay the patch clip and place it on the third layer, dropping the cutout to the fourth layer.
Align the patch clip to the same point so the layers line up without disturbing timing.
Add a rectangle mask to the patch clip to cover yourself, inverting it to position, opening it up, feathering the edges, then inverting again.
Reorder the cutout layer via Basic tab layer setting, setting it to layer 3.
Align all relevant layers to snap to the matching timing point.
At the point where the fly-up keyframe starts, set opacity keyframes on the patch layers so they don't fade before the keyframe begins.
Set opacity keyframes going from 0 to 100 so the patch layers fade in over the background.
Deselect and play back to review the fly-up animation.
Export the project as a pre-render.
Start a new project and add the exported clip.
Split the clip, then split again, and trim/delete the end.
Select the jump section and apply Motion Blur from the Basic tab, turning it all the way up.
Create a freeze frame of your body at the peak of the up movement (not coming back down).
Hide the bottom track via the eyeball icon, select the freeze frame, and apply Remove Background using auto removal.
On the freeze-frame cutout, go to Basic > Position, set a keyframe, move forward, and drag the figure straight up out of frame.
Adjust keyframe spacing to control fly-up speed, placing keyframes for a moderate pace.
Turn the original track back on.
From the original clip, find a frame where you are not in view, with clouds as close as possible to the action point.
Create a clean-plate freeze frame at that point, deleting the left and right sides.
Place the clean-plate freeze frame between the two clips, extending it to the end to preserve timing.
Arrange layers: original on bottom, clean plate in the middle, animated cutout on top.
In the Basic tab, scroll to layer order and set the clean plate to layer 2.
If the freeze was captured too late, re-create the freeze frame earlier while the figure is still moving up; duplicate, freeze, and delete left/right sides, extending to the end to keep timing.
Re-apply Remove Background to the new cutout using auto removal (or custom removal pro tool with a smaller brush for accuracy).
Set a position keyframe on the new cutout, move forward, and drag up while holding spacebar to lock vertical movement.
Fine-tune keyframe spacing for the desired fly-up speed.
Bring back the clean-plate freeze frame and reset it to layer 2.
Add a thin rectangle mask with feather on the clean plate to cover only the figure area while preserving the shadow.
Copy and paste the masked clean plate to make a second mask covering the ground shadow.
Move the animated freeze-frame cutout back to the top layer.
Play back to check for major issues.
For the spot where you walk out of frame uncovered by the mask, create another freeze frame, delete the extra, and drag it into place.
Add an ambient whoosh/shockwave sound effect and set its volume to about -5 dB as a subtle background.
Add a more prominent whoosh sound effect as the main accent.
Export the project as 'pre render flying version one'.
Import the pre-render back into the project on an upper track to keep manual placement control.
Mark the in point (I) at the first frame and the out point (O) at the end so export only renders that range.
Split the section that needs motion blur.
Select the correct clip and activate Motion Blur from the Basic tab, waiting for processing.
Play back to confirm smoothness, then export the final video.
Import the main portal footage into the project
Set the project aspect ratio to 9:16 vertical
Rotate the sideways DSLR footage to the correct upright orientation
Scale the footage up to fill the full vertical frame (around 178%)
Split the main clip after the opening forward step/movement
Import the portal asset (black backdrop) and add it as an overlay layer above the footage
Mirror/flip the portal overlay so it lines up with the shot
Set the portal overlay blend mode to Screen to remove the black background
Reposition and scale the portal overlay into place over the scene
Align the portal so its built-in reveal timing matches the action
Copy and paste a clean background segment (footage before the sign appears) and place it at the start as a patch layer
Add a mask to the patch layer using a rectangle/square shape to cover the held sign
Position the mask over the sign area to patch it out
Feather the mask edge to blend the patch and the arm smoothly
Scale the portal overlay a bit smaller for a more impactful look
Copy and paste the portal overlay onto a layer above itself to double up and brighten it
Keep the duplicated portal layer on Screen blend mode (test brighten/overlay/multiply but settle on Screen)
Fine-tune the patch mask feather (lower from 33) to remove the sharp line on the arm and blend inside the portal
Import the portal sound effect and add it to the audio track
Mute the original clip audio so background voices aren't heard
Duplicate the main footage onto a top layer and apply Remove Background using Custom Removal to cut out the arm
Apply the cutout so the arm appears in front of the portal
Add a rectangle mask to the cutout layer to reveal only the arm portion
Feather and position the cutout mask so the arm appears to reach through the portal
Split the music/audio, delete excess, and drag it to align with the end of the timeline
Rename the project using year-date plus a noun-verb description (e.g. 'Portal Grab')

recipes.title: Dr. Strange Portal
filming_steps: This effect we're gonna utilize our superhero powers.
Once again we're gonna use our tripod and our cell phone.
We're gonna set up our composition so that when we bring in our stock footage effect that we don't cross paths with the effects so we don't have to use any masking tools.
So that's a pretty nice composition there.
Use my card to mark a spot and see if I'm happy with that so I can see my card there I can see it's too close to the edge of the frame so I'm gonna scoot it over.
I'll put it here so that we can't see it but I'll note that I need to stand on that side of the card.
We're gonna activate AV Lock to lock our exposure and we'll hit record and just do a test shot see how it looks.
Let's take a look okay.
As you can see I'm a little too close to the edge of the frame so when we go to do our take I know to step in farther.
This will give us a whole open area on the right to add in our portal footage.
So block our exposure again.
Hit record and run in and do the same action.
All right let's see how that looks I think this will be the one.
We test the shot before we move our tripod and camera so that we don't have to set our equipment back up.

edit_steps.instruction: Import the running clip and the portal stock footage into the project.
Split the front of the running clip and delete the left portion so it starts on the run-in action.
Split the end of the running clip and trim off the right side.
Add the portal stock footage as an overlay layer and position it on the right side of the frame.
Select the portal overlay, open Blend, and set the blend mode to Filter (Screen).
Slide the portal overlay later in the timeline to adjust its timing.
Reposition and scale the portal so it sits on the ground in the distance and your hands don't cross over it.
Trim the end of the portal overlay and slide it so it ends at the exact same point as the running clip.
Optionally test alternate blend modes (Lighten, Dodge) and settle on Filter for the most realistic look.
In CapCut desktop, split the footage to start on a piece of action and delete the slow lead-in.
Bring in the portal as an overlay and set its blend mode from Default to Screen.
Adjust the portal overlay timing to align with the action.
For the simple version, shrink or scoot the portal so it doesn't overlap the person.
For the advanced version, copy and paste the base footage (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+V) and place the copy on the top layer to sandwich the portal between footage and a cutout.
Select the top footage layer and apply Remove Background using Auto removal to create a cutout of the person.
Set the layer order correctly: base footage layer 0, portal layer 1, and the cutout person as layer 2 so the person stays on top.
Play through, then trim the clip down to the desired end point.
Add a portal/electricity sound effect from CapCut's library as an audio track.
Lower the sound effect volume to around -5 to -8 dB.
Add a spark sound effect as a secondary layer to finish the effect.
Import your clip into CapCut.
Move the playhead to the point where the speed effect should begin and split the clip.
Move the playhead to the point where the speed effect should end and split the clip again.
Select the middle section and set its speed to 15x or higher.
With the middle section still selected, enable motion blur.
Boost the motion blur amount all the way up.
Set the motion blur blending to about 20.
Change the motion blur time setting to twice (2x) to double the blur amount.
Import the three shots into the project: the jump (action) shot, the clean plate cup shot, and the splash shot.
Place the jump/action clip on the lower track and stack the clean plate cup clip on the track above it.
Temporarily hide the upper cup layer by clicking its eyeball icon to focus on timing.
On the jump clip, find the frame where you disappear, split there, and delete the trailing portion you no longer need.
On the splash clip, step frame by frame with arrow keys to the first frame where the splash begins, split there, and delete the empty leading portion before the splash.
Snap the splash clip to butt directly against the end of the jump clip so they play seamlessly.
Turn the upper cup layer back on via the eyeball icon.
Free method: apply a rectangle mask to the cup clip and rotate it slightly to isolate the cup as a foreground cutout (positioned so the jumper clears the mask line).
Trim the start of the clip so the effect begins right when the action is about to happen, within the first two to three seconds.
Optionally add a teaser text overlay on screen (e.g. 'Coffee Hack') at the opening.
Delete the trimmed dead-space pieces and shift the remaining clip back so in/out points line up with the next clip.
To tighten the delay between the jump and the splash, split the jump clip at the chosen point.
Apply a speed change to the split segment (try 1.5x as a middle ground rather than 2.0x) to make the splash feel more natural.
Because speeding up shortens the clip, trim the tail of the matching layer back to keep both layers aligned.
Split off the end of the effect at the final intended point to mark where the main effect ends.
Pro method (preferred over the rectangle mask): select the cup clip and open Remove Background, then Custom Removal.
Use the Smart Brush to paint inside the cup and table to build the AI cutout of the cup as foreground.
Use the Smart Eraser to remove unwanted areas like the grass from the cutout selection.
Click Apply to commit the smart cutout for a cleaner edge than the rectangle mask.
To patch the grass that shifts during the transition, copy and paste the third (splash) clip to create a duplicate patch layer.
Apply a rectangle mask to the patch clip covering only the lower corner grass area that never moves.
Increase the mask feather to blend the patch seamlessly into the surrounding footage.
Search the Sound Effects panel for a water splash sound (e.g. 'running water splash') and add it to the timeline.
Position the splash sound effect to line up with the splash moment, nudging it slightly earlier as needed.

recipes.title: Wall Hologram
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard Screen-blend overlay method for holograms.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod facing the wall where the hologram will appear.
Act out reacting to / interacting with the empty wall so your eyeline and gestures sell it.

edit_steps.instruction: Import your main clip into the timeline
Add the hologram stock footage as an overlay layer above the main clip
Set the overlay's blend mode to Screen to drop the black background and keep only the glowing hologram
Scale and position the hologram overlay onto the wall
Add keyframes to track the hologram with camera or subject movement
Mask the overlay so part of the hologram is hidden behind you
Add a subtle blue glow to the hologram
Add a sci-fi sound effect

recipes.gear: Tripod, a wall/flat surface, hologram or HUD stock footage (e.g. free ProductionCrate elements).

recipes.title: 3 Clones
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip for this one — this is the standard locked-off cloning method that produces the exact result shown.
filming_steps: Mount your phone on a tripod and frame your scene. Do NOT move the camera between takes.
Take 1: stand/act in position A, then walk out of frame.
Take 2: without moving the camera, sit/act in position B, walk out.
Take 3: act in position C. Keep positions far enough apart that the clones don't overlap.
Use the same exposure/focus lock for every take so lighting matches.

edit_steps.instruction: Import all 3 clips into CapCut
Place clip 1 on the main track
Add clip 2 as an overlay layer above the main track
Add clip 3 as an overlay layer above clip 2
Add a Rectangle or Brush mask to overlay 2 covering the area where that clone sits
Feather the mask edge slightly on overlay 2
Add a Rectangle or Brush mask to overlay 3 covering its clone area
Feather the mask edge slightly on overlay 3
Trim the clips so the action lines up across all three layers
Add audio to the project

recipes.gear: Phone on a tripod (locked off — never move it), good even lighting.

recipes.title: House Pond
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard masked water-overlay method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod looking at the patch of ground/yard you want to turn into water.

edit_steps.instruction: Add the water/pond stock footage as an overlay layer above the base shot
Apply a mask to the water overlay shaped to the area that should become the pond
Feather the mask edge so the water blends into the ground
Lower the opacity of the water overlay slightly to blend with the ground
Match the color of the water overlay to the surrounding ground
Duplicate the scenery clip to create a reflection layer
Flip the duplicated reflection layer vertically
Mask the flipped reflection into the water area
Add ripple/ambient water sound to the project

recipes.gear: Tripod, water/pond or reflection stock footage.

recipes.title: Storm Clouds
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard sky-replacement method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera on a tripod with the sky visible in frame.
A static shot makes the sky replacement far easier.

edit_steps.instruction: Add the storm-cloud footage as an overlay layer above your main clip
Mask the overlay to the sky region to replace the existing sky, or use Cutout if there's a clean horizon
Set blend mode and opacity so the new clouds sit naturally over the original
Keyframe a slow drift on the cloud overlay
Apply a cool color grade to the shot
Add wind and thunder sound effects

recipes.gear: Tripod, storm-cloud / time-lapse sky stock footage.

recipes.title: Building Jump
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard match-cut jump method.
filming_steps: Location A: film yourself crouching and JUMPING UP, exiting the top of frame.
Location B: film yourself DROPPING INTO frame and landing, matching the same body angle.
Match framing and motion direction at the cut point (the apex of the jump).

edit_steps.instruction: Place clip A on the timeline first
Place clip B on the timeline after clip A
Trim the end of clip A to the moment you leave the top of frame
Trim the start of clip B to the moment you enter from the top of frame
Butt the clips together at the apex to form the match cut into one continuous super-jump
Add a whoosh/impact SFX aligned to the landing
Apply a subtle speed ramp around the cut for added punch

recipes.gear: Two locations (e.g. ground + rooftop/ledge), tripod or steady hands.

recipes.title: 3 Product Reveal
recipes.summary: The vault ships only an inspiration reel here — this is the standard beat-synced reveal method.
filming_steps: Film a short clip of each product (or each angle) with identical framing and lighting.
Add a consistent motion you can transition on — e.g. swipe a hand across, or a quick push toward the lens.

edit_steps.instruction: Place the three product clips in sequence on the timeline
Add a trending audio track and align it to the timeline
Trim and sync each clip's cut points to the audio beats
Add a quick zoom/spin/swipe transition between each clip, timed to the motion or beat
Add a text/price callout on each product reveal
Import the sky background clip and place it on the base video track
Add a text layer with 'SKY TRANSITION DEMO' centered on screen
Set the title font to bold and color to white
Apply a transition between the sky scenes

recipes.title: Sky Transition
recipes.summary: The vault ships only a demo clip here — this is the standard whip-pan sky transition.
filming_steps: Scene 1: whip-pan UP to the sky (fast tilt) at the end of the shot.
Scene 2: start on the sky and whip-pan DOWN into the new location.
Keep the pan speed/direction matching between the two.

edit_steps.instruction: Add scene 1 clip to the timeline
Place scene 2 clip after scene 1 on the timeline
Split scene 1 at the fastest, most-blurred sky-pointing frame of the upward pan and trim off the excess
Split scene 2 at the fastest, most-blurred sky-pointing frame of the downward pan and trim off the excess
Add a quick blur or directional-blur transition over the cut between the two clips
Optionally mask the sky on the new scene so its sky bleeds in early over the cut

recipes.gear: Phone, two scenes you want to connect.

recipes.title: Object Switch
recipes.summary: The vault marks the full tutorial 'coming soon' — this is the standard locked-off switch method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera. Hold object A still in frame.
Pass a hand / quick motion across the object, OR briefly cover it.
Without moving the camera, swap to object B in the exact same position and repeat the motion.

edit_steps.instruction: Place object A clip and object B clip on the timeline
Split clip A at the frame where the hand fully covers the object
Split clip B at the matching frame where the hand reveals the object
Align object B's clip so the hand reveal lines up with the wipe moment for a seamless switch
Add a magic/whoosh SFX at the switch point

recipes.title: Moving Object
recipes.summary: The vault marks the full tutorial 'coming soon' — this is the standard keyframed clean-plate method.
filming_steps: Lock the camera and film the scene WITH the object sitting still.
Film a clean plate of the same scene WITHOUT the object (for patching).
Optionally film yourself reacting to the 'moving' object.

edit_steps.instruction: Place the clean plate clip on the main video track.
Add the object clip as an overlay layer above the clean plate.
Mask out just the object on the overlay so only it shows over the clean plate.
Add Position keyframes on the object overlay to animate it sliding across the scene.
Apply ease to the position keyframes for a creeping, organic motion.
Add a subtle shadow that moves with the object using keyframes.
Add an eerie sound effect on the audio track.

recipes.gear: Tripod (locked off), the object, a clean-plate shot.

recipes.title: Your 1st Cloning Video
filming_steps: Anytime I come out to shoot the first thing I'm gonna think about is the lighting situation.
I've chosen a nice shady spot to help avoid the shadows and harsh lighting.
We're gonna find a nice central location to set up our tripod.
We're recording with our phone so we're gonna set this on the tripod and get our composition, the way that we like it.
Nice wide frame and what I'm gonna do is I'm going to take one long shot.
We go ahead and record here.
I'm gonna test my composition first and what I wanna do is find two places and use a marker I'm using playing cards here for this demonstration.
I'm gonna place them on the ground.
I'm gonna come back and look at my camera to review the spots and see if that's what I'm happy with I'm gonna shift my tripod to the right a little bit and make a slight adjustment.

I know that when I shoot my clone video one clone will be on this side the other clone will be on that side and I can just kind of leave those markers there just to make sure I don't cross and I can see them out of the corner of my eye without having to look at the post.

So gonna come over here and stop recording and then I'll start what I wanna always do is hold my finger down on the ground to activate a V lock that's gonna make it so that the lighting doesn't shift over time, the exposure and the focus is all locked.
We're gonna hit record and we will call this shot one.

I'm gonna walk into the scene and I'm gonna interact with my clone and I'm gonna run to this side continue recording and we're gonna call this shot 2 and then this clone is gonna react to that clone and then I and then we'll come over here and we will stop recording and review our footage.
Speed through it make sure I'm standing in a good spot, for this one and yeah looks good.
We can split it right down the middle no issues.
You can see there's no problems with the shadows on the ground because we're in the shade.
We'll come clean up our mess.
So this is a simple cloning technique where we're using two shots to combine them into one shot using a simple mask that's gonna be split down the middle.
Let's go check it out and then edit.

edit_steps.instruction: Create a new project in CapCut.
Import the source clip into the project.
Scroll to the start of shot one, select the clip, and split it.
Delete the unwanted left segment before shot one.
Scroll forward to the start of shot two and split the clip there.
Delete the left segment you don't need.
Scroll forward to the end of shot two and split again.
Delete the unwanted right segment, leaving two clips.
Select the second clip and tap overlay to move it onto its own layer.
Long press the overlay clip and drag it left to align it with the first clip so both play simultaneously.
Select the overlay clip, open opacity, and bring it down to 50% to see both motions at once.
Slide the overlay clip left or right to fine-tune timing alignment between the two clones.
Once timing looks good, bring the overlay opacity back up to 100%.
With the overlay clip selected, tap Mask and choose the Split mask.
Rotate the mask vertically and position the split line down the middle along the pole to hide the seam.
Drag the white feather arrow to add a slight feather and blend the two clips together.

A shot of us pouring the coffee into the cup and then a shot of us setting the phone on top of the cup, pushing down and we're gonna combine the two shots to make it feel like we're pushing the coffee into the cup.
This is gonna be our coffee hack.
So let's go ahead and lock our exposure.
Lock it on the table on the countertop right next to the glass and hit record, okay.
I got my phone the picture of my coffee beans here.
They show up good and I have my coffee right here.
I'm actually gonna do the coffee shot first and I wanna make sure none of my arms or shadows cross over anything as much as possible.
To make it easier to do the masking.
Looks good.
It's gonna be easier I'm gonna use this to pour.
So my light is coming from that way.
I'm gonna stand on pour it from this side so I don't get any shadows and my mask will sit there 1 2 3 So I purposefully poured it a little bit fast.
I think that's gonna work better for the effect.
So now let's get our second shot and I wanna make sure that I don't stand behind the glass cause we can see through it.
So we're still recording and this coffee hack.
So I did that I counted four seconds.
I'm gonna do another one.
Coffee hack.
That one was 6 seconds.
Just for safe measure make sure you're recording and just for safe measure I'm gonna go ahead and get a third one so I don't have to set all this stuff up again.
Coffee hack.
I'm gonna go ahead and watch those back.
I just to be clear I did one for four seconds one for six seconds that one I did for about eight seconds.
What I'm watching for is just when it's playing when I'm holding the phone I wanna make sure that I don't go behind the coffee.
Cause that's gonna mess up the illusion.
Yeah I think it looks great.
Let's go edit this thing.

edit_steps.instruction: Create a new project in CapCut.
Import the source clip containing both the coffee pour and phone touch shots.
Drop the imported clip into the timeline.
Split at the start of the coffee pour section and delete everything to the left.
Split at the end of the coffee pour (leave a little extra), keeping this as the coffee pour clip.
Locate the phone-touch take (the second take with the finger), split at its start and delete everything to the left.
Split at the end of the phone-touch clip and delete everything to the right, leaving just the coffee pour and the phone touch clips.
Place the coffee pour clip as an overlay layer on top of the phone-touch (main) layer.
Select the coffee pour overlay and lower its opacity to 50% (Video > Basic) so you can see through to line up timing.
On the main clip, split at the moment the fingers push down to create a reference mark.
Drag the coffee pour overlay so the pour aligns with the push-down reference mark; nudge it slightly earlier until the timing feels right.
Select the coffee pour clip and turn its opacity back up to 100%.
With the coffee pour clip selected, open the Mask tool and apply a rectangle mask.
Shape and shrink the rectangle mask to sit right below the phone and fit around the glass without covering the phone.
Add a touch of feather (around 2-3) via the mask adjust settings to blend the mask edge seamlessly.
To remove your talking at the head of the clip, set a volume keyframe (or split and lower volume) so the audio starts cleanly at the pour.
To cover the hand crossing the frame, duplicate the clip and create a freeze frame at the moment before the hand enters.
Trim the freeze frame to cover the cup only up to the needed point, starting it at the end of the previous clip.
Fade the freeze-frame clip in (and out) using opacity keyframes to eliminate the hard-cut pop.

Our pan and a soft cloth to catch the ball.
When actually move the handle out of the way so that it doesn't cross over our body in the camera's view.
Maybe right there.

So basically what we're gonna do is bounce the ping pong ball and have it stop and land we don't want it to bounce out and then we're gonna transition to cracking the egg and it's gonna look like the ball turns into the egg.
We're gonna record.
Looks good.
We'll establish the ball by bouncing it first.
Breakfast hack Do a couple takes so I can choose the best one.
Breakfast hack Breakfast hack Okay think that was the one.
I'll get one more just in case.
Breakfast hack Breakfast hack Cool I'm gonna go ahead and pause.
What I need to do is heat up the pan really hot.
That when I crack the egg we hear that sizzle right away and it starts cooking.
Gonna give a better effect.
I'm gonna go ahead and pause.
Let this heat up for a minute and I'll be right back.
That should be hot enough.
Since I didn't clear out of the screen the exposure and focus lock is in the same place.
All I have to do is just hit record and be careful not to bump the camera too much.

Gonna go ahead and crack my egg drop it on gonna make sure to clear all my arms and any shadows that I'll create as I'm cracking the egg so that when we blend the two clips together that there's no mistakes with the masking.
A lot of shadows.

edit_steps.instruction: Import the two recorded clips into the project.
On the ball clip, split at the point just before the bounce and delete the left side to remove everything before that part.
Split the ball clip after your reaction and delete the right side to trim off the extra takes.
On the egg clip, leave some extra footage at the beginning to cover what happened in the ball clip, then split and delete the left side.
Select the egg clip and add it as an overlay on its own layer.
Shorten and reposition the egg overlay clip to roughly align it with the ball clip.
Lower the egg overlay opacity to 50% so both clips are visible for timing alignment.
Align the timing so the egg hits exactly when the ball bounces and lands; zoom in and extend the front as needed for precision.
Turn the egg overlay opacity back up to 100%.
Add a circle mask to the egg overlay clip, sized to match the pan.
Position and scale the circle mask to cover the inside of the pan and hide the napkin.
Increase the mask feather to blend the two scenes together believably.

recipes.title: Magic Pole Transition
filming_steps: For this transition we have our phone in our tripod.
We have our pole in the center and we have our two outfits.
I'm gonna use my two t-shirts to transition but you can use whatever outfits you want to.
So as always we're gonna wanna use our AV lock to lock the exposure so we don't get any lighting shifts.
Right go ahead and hit record so for a transition like this we want to keep the move very simple okay so I'll make a mental note of where I'm standing and I'm just gonna do one step acrossed.
Now I'm gonna go back and try to stand in the same spot as I was before and I'm gonna change my shirt.
I have my blue shirt and I'm gonna take the same step across the pole.
My shirt's blue depending on the effect that you're trying to achieve you could just jump across.
So I'll do the same thing change my shirt back.
All right and then I'm gonna make the same jump across.

So when it happens quick like that sometimes it's a lot easier to hide the transition because it's happening so quick that the eye doesn't catch it but if you're moving slowly past your pole or whatever you're using as your object it's gonna be a lot easier to notice the imperfections.

edit_steps.instruction: Import both trimmed clips and drag the footage down into the timeline.
Split and trim the first clip to isolate the red shirt shot, keeping it short.
Trim the second clip to isolate the blue shirt shot.
Select the blue shirt clip and tap Overlay to drop it onto its own layer stacked on top of the red clip.
With the blue clip selected, open the Basic tab and lower its opacity to 50% so both layers are visible.
Slip the blue layer left/right so the step across the pole lines up with the red layer at the same moment the foot crosses.
Zoom in and nudge the blue layer one more frame to fine-tune the timing match.
Drag the blue layer all the way to the front so there is no popping or gap and the timing lines up.
Trim the clips so both layers are the same length.
Turn the blue layer's opacity back up to 100%.
With the blue (top) clip selected, open the Mask tool and choose the horizontal/split mask.
Rotate the mask line to 90 degrees (vertical) so blue shows on the right and red on the left.
Position the mask line in the center, hidden inside the pole.
Increase the mask feather to about 3 to soften the harsh edge line.
Shift the blue layer one more frame and pull its edge back a frame to refine the timing, then review.
